Not an official carrier short code
They triggered the code, now want you to confirm it

explanation.short: The scammer triggered a verification code to be sent to your phone, and now wants you to hand it back to them.
tells: Your carrier never asks you to reply with a verification code — they sent it to confirm you have the phone
This is an account takeover attempt: the code they want IS your account's security code
Real carrier service issues don't threaten 1-hour suspension via text
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. Call your carrier directly using the number on your bill or carrier's official website.
explanation.consequence: Replying with the code hands the scammer control of your phone number — enabling SIM swap to hijack all your accounts.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never reply to a text with a code you received — anyone asking for a code you were just sent is trying to take over your account.

message.subject: Final Notice: Vehicle Registration Overdue — Fee Increasing
message.body: Your vehicle registration has expired. A late fee of $47.00 has been applied. Pay the total of $139.00 online before the end of this month to avoid your registration being flagged and your vehicle becoming unregisterable. Pay at: dmv-registration-services.com/pay

explanation.short: Your state DMV uses a .gov domain — any DMV email from a .com is a fake payment page.
tells: All state DMV offices use [state].gov domains — no legitimate DMV uses dmv-registration-services.com
Real registration renewals are mailed or accessible through your state's official DMV website
The payment amount and urgency are designed to feel routine enough to not question
explanation.safe_move: Visit your state's official DMV website (e.g., dmv.ca.gov, nydmv.ny.gov) directly to check your registration status.
explanation.consequence: The payment page captures your card details — no registration is renewed, and the money goes to scammers.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government fees are always paid through official .gov websites — never through .com links in emails.

message.body: NorthStar Bank: Did you attempt a $412.00 purchase at GardenMart in Phoenix, AZ? Reply YES to approve or NO to decline and freeze card. Msg&Data rates may apply.

red_flags.label: Asks you to reply YES or NO

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank fraud alert — real bank texts come from short codes (5-6 digits), not 10-digit numbers, and only ask YES or NO.
tells: Legitimate bank fraud alerts come from 5-digit short codes like 63775, not 10-digit numbers
The message asks only YES or NO — it does not ask for account numbers, passwords, or to call a separate number
No link is included — real fraud alerts don't need you to click anything
explanation.safe_move: Reply YES if you made the purchase, NO if you didn't. If NO, your bank will call you from their official number.
explanation.consequence: Not responding to a real fraud alert may result in legitimate purchases being blocked.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Real bank fraud alerts use short codes and only ask YES or NO — they never include links or ask you to call an unknown number.

message.body: SwiftShip: Your package (tracking #SW8842917) is out for delivery today by 8pm. Track at swiftship.com/track or reply STOP to opt out of alerts.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate delivery notification — short code sender, official domain, no payment or login requested.
tells: Comes from a short code (48773), which carriers register with wireless providers
The tracking link goes to swiftship.com — the carrier's official domain, not a lookalike
No payment, no clicking a link to 'release' the package, no fee requested
explanation.safe_move: You can track the package at swiftship.com using the tracking number provided.
explanation.consequence: No action needed unless you want to track the package.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate delivery texts use short codes and direct you to the carrier's real website — no fees, no pressure.

message.subject: Your annual membership renews in 30 days — here's what's included
message.body:
Hi Dorothy,

Your Reader's Companion membership (Plan: Annual Digital) renews on March 15 for $39.99. No action needed — we'll charge the card ending in 4471 on file.

To manage your subscription, visit readerscompanion.com/account or call us at 1-800-555-0221.

Thank you for being a member!

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal notice — it comes from the company's real domain, shows your card's last 4 digits, and gives 30 days' notice.
tells: Sent from readerscompanion.com — the company's actual domain
Shows your card's last 4 digits as confirmation you're an existing customer — scammers don't know this
30-day advance notice is standard practice for legitimate renewals
Provides both a web address and phone number for managing your subscription
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if you want to keep the subscription. Visit readerscompanion.com/account to cancel or update your plan.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine renewal notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A legitimate renewal shows your last 4 card digits and gives ample notice — fake billing notices create panic with surprise charges.

message.subject: Your GuardMax subscription renewed successfully
message.body:
Hi Robert,

Your GuardMax Total Protection subscription has been renewed for another year. You were charged $89.99 on your Visa ending in 7823.

No action is needed. Your devices remain protected.

If you have questions, visit guardmax.com/support or call 1-800-555-0189.

The GuardMax Team

red_flags.label: Charge already applied to your card

explanation.short: This is a legitimate renewal confirmation — it comes from guardmax.com and shows your actual card details.
tells: Sent from guardmax.com — not guardmax-renewal-center.com or any hyphenated fake
Confirms the last 4 digits of the card on file — a real company knows this, scammers don't
No phone number to 'cancel' a surprise charge — just their standard support line
No urgency, no threats, no links to 'verify' anything
explanation.safe_move: No action needed if you have GuardMax and recognize this subscription.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a confirmation of a known subscription.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate billing emails confirm card details you already know — they don't create panic about unexpected charges.

message.subject: We noticed a new sign-in to your account
message.body:
Hi Margaret,

We noticed a sign-in to your NovaSoft account from a new device (iPhone, Chicago IL) on Feb 14 at 2:11 PM.

If this was you, no action is needed.

If this wasn't you, secure your account at novasoft.com/security — do not click any other links.

NovaSoft Security Team

red_flags.label: Sign-in from a city you don't recognize
Link to click in security email

explanation.short: This is a legitimate security notification from NovaSoft — it comes from novasoft.com and tells you to go to the real site, not a link.
tells: Sent from account-security@novasoft.com — the company's actual domain
Crucially, the email says 'do not click any other links' and directs you to the main site
If it were you signing in (e.g., you got a new phone), this is just routine
Real security alerts don't include 'verify identity' links — they direct you to the main website
explanation.safe_move: If the sign-in was you, no action needed. If not, go to novasoft.com directly and change your password.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email — but if someone else signed in, change your password promptly.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A legitimate security alert directs you to the main website — it does not include a 'verify here' link in the email body.

message.subject: Unusual activity on your account — please review
message.body:
Dear Helen,

We noticed a transaction that may be unusual for your account:

$214.00 — TechGadgets Online — Feb 12

If you recognize this transaction, no action is needed. If you don't, please call the number on the back of your debit card or log in at bluepeak.com to dispute it.

Do not reply to this email.

BluePeak Fraud Prevention

explanation.short: This is a legitimate fraud alert from BluePeak — it comes from bluepeak.com and directs you to call the number on your card, not a link.
tells: Sent from alerts@bluepeak.com — the bank's official domain
Directs you to the number on your card or bluepeak.com — not a separate support number
Says 'do not reply to this email' — a real security measure, not a redirect to a scam phone line
No login link embedded in the email, no urgency, no threat of account closure
explanation.safe_move: If you recognize the transaction, no action needed. If not, call the number on your card.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Acting on it promptly (if the charge is fraudulent) protects your account.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank fraud emails reference your card's back number — they don't embed a call-now link to an unknown number.

message.subject: Overdue items on your library account
message.body:
Dear Patron,

You have overdue items on your library account:

- 'The Garden of Lost Souls' (due Jan 28) — $0.50/day

Please return the item or renew online at citylibraryohio.gov/account. Your total fine is currently $4.00.

Thank you,
City Library System

red_flags.label: Library fine you may not have expected

explanation.short: This is a legitimate library overdue notice — it comes from a .gov domain and contains specific, accurate account details.
tells: Sent from citylibraryohio.gov — a government domain, consistent with a public library
Contains specific book title and a small, realistic fine amount
Directs you to the library's own website — not a third-party payment link
No urgency, no threats, no unusual requests
explanation.safe_move: Return the book or renew at citylibraryohio.gov. The fine is small and easy to pay through the official site.
explanation.consequence: No risk — just a routine overdue notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government and public institution emails use .gov domains and include specific details matching your actual account.

message.body: Medicare: Your annual wellness visit reminder is due. Schedule at medicare.gov or call 1-800-MEDICARE. Reply STOP to opt out of reminders.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate Medicare wellness reminder — it uses a short code and directs you only to medicare.gov or the official 1-800-MEDICARE line.
tells: Comes from a short code (77969), consistent with official government notification programs
Only directs to medicare.gov and the official 1-800-MEDICARE number — no other links
No request for payment, personal information, or urgency
Offers opt-out via STOP — standard for legitimate government SMS programs
explanation.safe_move: Schedule your wellness visit at medicare.gov if you haven't had one this year.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a health reminder you can act on or ignore.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Government texts from short codes directing only to .gov websites and known numbers are legitimate — scam texts use 10-digit numbers and sketchy links.

message.subject: Your PeachDevice account has been locked — call us now
message.body: We have locked your PeachDevice account after detecting unauthorized access. To unlock your account and recover your photos, call our Senior Assistance Line at 1-888-555-0774. Our technicians are available 24/7. Do not attempt to log in until you call us.

red_flags.label: Unsolicited account lock with call-now instruction

explanation.short: PeachDevice sends account notices from peachdevice.com — this domain is fake, and the 'Senior Assistance Line' is a social engineering signal.
tells: peachdevice-help-center.net is not peachdevice.com — always check the full domain
A 'Senior Assistance Line' label is used to target older adults specifically
'Do not log in until you call' prevents you from verifying the claim yourself
Real PeachDevice account issues are resolved at appleid.apple.com directly
explanation.safe_move: Ignore the email. Go to peachdevice.com directly and sign in to check your account status.
explanation.consequence: Calling connects you to scammers who ask for remote device access — they will steal your account and financial information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When told not to log in and to call instead, that's the scam — always verify account issues yourself through the official website.

message.subject: Congratulations! You've been selected for a remote position — $85/hr
message.body: Hi! We reviewed your profile and you're a great fit for our Remote Data Entry Specialist role. Starting pay is $85/hr, fully remote, flexible hours. No experience necessary — we provide full training. Reply with your full name, address, and date of birth to begin the onboarding process.

red_flags.label: Requests full name, address, DOB upfront

explanation.short: No legitimate employer pays $85/hr for no-experience data entry — and real jobs never request your date of birth over email.
tells: $85/hr data entry with no experience is not a real market rate — it's designed to attract and then exploit
Legitimate employers collect personal information through secure onboarding portals, not email replies
You were 'selected' without applying or interviewing — real hiring processes require this
The domain talenthub-careers.net is not the official TalentHub platform
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply. Research TalentHub directly at talenthub.com and report the solicitation as spam.
explanation.consequence: Your PII (name, address, DOB) is used for identity theft, or you'll receive a fraudulent check as part of a check overpayment scam.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited job offers with high pay and no interview are bait — real employers post jobs and hold interviews.

message.subject: Welcome aboard — your first check is attached
message.body: Congratulations on joining our remote bookkeeping team! Your signing bonus of $3,800 is attached as a check. Please deposit it and use $2,200 to purchase office supplies from our approved vendor (we'll send the link). Keep the remaining $1,600 as your first week's advance. Let us know once deposited!

red_flags.label: Deposit check and buy supplies — classic check scam
Told to keep part of the check

explanation.short: This is a check overpayment scam — the check will bounce after you've already spent $2,200 on fake 'supplies.'
tells: Legitimate employers never send checks before you start and ask you to forward money elsewhere
The check will appear to clear initially, but bounce 1–2 weeks later — you're liable for the full amount
The 'approved vendor' is controlled by the scammer — your $2,200 goes directly to them
No real employer onboards a new hire via email check attachment
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any check. Report the email as fraud and block the sender.
explanation.consequence: You deposit, spend $2,200, then the check bounces. Your bank holds you responsible for the negative balance.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that mails you a check before you start and asks you to send part of it elsewhere is always a scam.

message.from_name: Sarah Chen — Talent Partner at Apex
message.body: Hi! I came across your profile and think you'd be a great fit for a Senior UX Role at Apex Technologies ($130k+, remote). We're moving fast on this — can you hop on a quick CloudMeet today? I'll send a link. Just need a soft copy of your resume to share with the hiring manager first.

red_flags.label: Recruiter contacting via personal DM handle

explanation.short: Legitimate corporate recruiters contact candidates through verified company accounts or official platforms — not personal-looking DM handles.
tells: A real Apex Technologies recruiter would message from a verified company profile or email
'Moving fast' is urgency designed to prevent you from verifying the recruiter's identity
Sending your resume in DM bypasses any official application system — it may be harvested for identity theft
The handle @sarah.chen.apex.recruit is a personal-style account, not a verified company presence
explanation.safe_move: Don't send your resume. Search for the role on Apex Technologies' official site. If interested, apply through the official channel.
explanation.consequence: Your resume contains your home address, phone, and work history — valuable for identity theft or targeted spear phishing.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Verify recruiter identity through the company's official website before sharing any documents.

message.subject: Action Required: Purchase your remote work equipment
message.body:
Welcome to BrightPath! Before your start date, you'll need to purchase your home office equipment through our certified supplier. Total cost: $847. You'll be fully reimbursed on your first paycheck. Purchase link: brightpath-equip.com/order

Please complete this within 48 hours to hold your position.

red_flags.label: Equipment from a separate third-party link

explanation.short: Legitimate remote employers ship equipment to you or reimburse through official expense systems — they never ask new hires to buy from a third-party link.
tells: Real companies either ship hardware directly or set up corporate accounts — they don't ask new hires to buy equipment out-of-pocket upfront
The reimbursement promise disappears once you pay — you will never hear from 'BrightPath' again
The equipment link (brightpath-equip.com) is a separate domain from the employer — a fake vendor
.co domains are cheap and commonly used for job scams
explanation.safe_move: Do not purchase anything. Verify the company exists at brightpathsolutions.com or CareerNet. Contact them through an independently verified number.
explanation.consequence: You pay $847, the equipment never arrives, and the 'company' disappears. The money is unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job that asks you to pay for equipment before starting — with a reimbursement promise — is a scam.

message.subject: 3 employers viewed your profile — verify to see who
message.body:
Great news! 3 recruiters viewed your JobBridge profile in the last 24 hours. Log in to see who's interested and send them a message before they move on. Your profile visibility expires in 12 hours without verification.

Verify and see who viewed you: jobbridge-verify.com/profile

red_flags.label: Must verify via link to see results

explanation.short: The domain is a lookalike — the real JobBridge sends alerts from jobbridge.com, not jobbridge-notifications.com.
tells: jobbridge-notifications.com is not jobbridge.com — real platforms send from their own domain
'Profile visibility expires in 12 hours' is fabricated urgency — job platforms don't delete profiles this way
The 'verify here' link leads to a fake login page to capture your credentials
Curiosity about who viewed you is the emotional hook to make you click without checking the URL
explanation.safe_move: Go to jobbridge.com directly and log in there to check profile views.
explanation.consequence: The fake login page captures your JobBridge username and password — the scammer can then access your profile and apply to jobs as you.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: When a job platform 'notification' asks you to verify, always go directly to the site — never through the email link.

message.subject: Earn $500–$800/week stuffing envelopes from home
message.body:
We are looking for motivated individuals to earn $500–$800 weekly stuffing envelopes at home. No experience needed. Start immediately. There is a small $25 registration/materials fee to receive your starter kit. Pay at the link below and we'll ship your kit within 3 days.

earnathhome-register.com/start

red_flags.label: Envelope stuffing is not a real paid job

explanation.short: Envelope stuffing jobs have been a well-known scam for decades — no one pays $800/week for this, and any job requiring a fee to start is a scam.
tells: Legitimate jobs never require you to pay a fee to receive work or materials
Envelope stuffing as paid employment does not exist at scale — it's always a scam front
The .biz domain and generic 'HR Department' sender indicate no real company
The fee is the entire point — you pay $25, receive nothing meaningful, and the scammers move on
explanation.safe_move: Ignore and delete. Report as job scam if you received it through a job platform.
explanation.consequence: You pay $25, receive worthless 'materials' or nothing at all, and the 'company' is unreachable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job requiring you to pay a fee to get started — however small — is a scam. Legitimate employers pay you, not the reverse.

message.body: Hey! I manage social media for several brands and I'm looking for brand ambassadors. You'd post 2–3 times a week about products and earn $300–$500/week. Just need to buy a starter product bundle at a discount ($129) to get familiar with the line. DM me 'INTERESTED' to start!

red_flags.label: Must buy products to become an ambassador
Offer comes from personal account, not brand

explanation.short: Real brand ambassadors are not required to purchase products upfront — any arrangement requiring payment to work is an MLM or scam.
tells: Legitimate brand partnerships provide products for free as part of a paid arrangement
Paying $129 for a 'starter bundle' is the actual business model — your income is a fiction
Real brand ambassador programs are run through official company channels, not personal DMs
The income promise ($300–$500/week) is unverifiable and designed to justify the upfront cost
explanation.safe_move: Do not pay. Look up the brand's official ambassador program at their company website if interested.
explanation.consequence: You pay $129 for products you may not be able to sell, and the promised income never materializes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any job offer that requires you to buy something first is either a scam or an MLM — neither are reliable income.

message.subject: You are hired as a Mystery Shopper — check enclosed
message.body: Congratulations! You have been selected as a Mystery Shopper for our national retail audit program. Your assignment kit and $2,950 check will arrive by SwiftShip within 3 days. Deposit the check, keep $350 as your fee, and use the remainder to evaluate MoneyGram services at Walmart. Send the evaluation via the included form.

red_flags.label: Deposit check and wire money elsewhere
Job involves wiring money via MoneyGram
Keep a cut from the check

explanation.short: Mystery shopper check scams are one of the most common job frauds — the check bounces after you've already wired $2,600.
tells: Real mystery shopping pays $10–$25 per assignment — not $350 plus access to thousands of dollars
Legitimate mystery shoppers are never asked to evaluate wire transfer services with their own money
The check will clear initially but bounce 1–2 weeks later — you are liable for the wire you sent
Selected without applying or any interview process is a universal scam signal
explanation.safe_move: Do not deposit any unsolicited check. Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov.
explanation.consequence: You send $2,600 via MoneyGram before the check bounces. That money is gone permanently.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Mystery shopping jobs that mail you a check and ask you to wire money are always scams — 100% of the time.

message.subject: Employment Agreement — Please Review and Sign
message.body:
Please find attached your employment agreement for the Remote Project Coordinator role. Review the document, enable macros if prompted to view the full contract, and reply with your signed copy.

Start date: March 1. We look forward to welcoming you to the team!

red_flags.label: Contract sent without prior interview contact

explanation.short: Enabling macros in a Word document from an unknown sender is one of the most common ways malware is installed.
tells: 'Enable macros to view the full document' is a standard malware delivery technique — real contracts don't require macros
Legitimate employment contracts are sent via SignVault-style portals, not macro-enabled Word files
The .co domain is cheap and easy to register — commonly used in impersonation attacks
You have no prior relationship with 'Meridian Group' to explain this contract
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment or enable macros. Research the company independently and contact them through an official channel.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros runs hidden code that installs a keylogger or remote access tool on your computer.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enable macros in an Office document you received unexpectedly — this is almost always malware delivery.

message.body: CareerLink: Your application has been reviewed and you've been shortlisted for a remote role paying $72/hr. Complete your profile at: careerlink-apply.net/shortlist?ref=7829

red_flags.label: May not have applied to this

explanation.short: A $72/hr shortlisting text from a random number pointing to a lookalike domain is credential harvesting, not a job offer.
tells: CareerLink would message from a short code or from careerlink.com — not a 10-digit number
careerlink-apply.net is not careerlink.com — the hyphenated domain is a fake
No role title, no company name, no reason for the high rate — real shortlist emails include specifics
The link contains a tracking ref parameter designed to associate your click with your phone number
explanation.safe_move: Log in to CareerLink directly at careerlink.com to check your applications and messages.
explanation.consequence: The link leads to a fake login page — your credentials are captured and your job platform account is taken over.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Job platform notifications should always be verified by logging in to the platform directly, never through a link in a text.

message.subject: Authorize access to complete your application
message.body:
To complete your application for the Data Analyst role, please authorize our HireNow portal to access your CloudSync account. This is required to verify your work history documents.

Authorize: hirenow-portal.co/oauth/cloudsync

This process takes under 2 minutes.

red_flags.label: Job application requires cloud account access

explanation.short: No legitimate job portal requires access to your cloud storage account — this is an OAuth token theft attempt.
tells: Employers verify work history via references and resume documents — never by accessing your personal cloud
Authorizing the OAuth link gives the fake portal read/write access to all your cloud files
The .co domain is a budget registration typically avoided by established companies
Real applicant portals are established brands (Greenhouse, Lever, Workday) with verifiable company ownership
explanation.safe_move: Do not authorize. Research the company independently. Submit documents as PDF attachments through official channels.
explanation.consequence: The OAuth grant gives attackers access to your entire cloud account — files, emails, and anything synced.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never authorize third-party apps to access your accounts during a job application — no legitimate employer requires this.

message.body: Hey! I saw you're in finance too — small world! I've been doing freelance consulting while also investing. My mentor taught me a crypto strategy that's been life-changing. I was skeptical too but I've made $18k this month. Happy to show you how it works over a call sometime if you're curious? No pressure at all!

red_flags.label: Stranger offering investment advice via DM

explanation.short: This is the opening move of a pig-butchering scam — build trust over weeks, then introduce a 'mentor' and a fake crypto platform.
tells: The script follows pig-butchering exactly: shared interest → casual conversation → mention of crypto gains → offer to explain
The 'mentor' is the next step — eventually you'll be invited to invest on a platform the mentor 'recommends'
$18k/month claims are unverifiable and are used to establish credibility without evidence
'No pressure at all' is a disarming phrase designed to make you lower skepticism
explanation.safe_move: Do not engage further. Block the account. Report it as a romance/investment scam on the platform.
explanation.consequence: Engaging leads to a weeks-long relationship that ends with you investing on a fake exchange where your funds are unwithdrawable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Strangers who mention crypto gains and offer to teach you their 'strategy' are running pig-butchering scams — 100% of the time.

message.subject: Your Glassdoor review was flagged — verify account to avoid suspension
message.body:
A recent review you posted may violate our community guidelines. To prevent account suspension, please verify your identity at the link below within 48 hours.

Verify: glassdoor-jobs.net/verify-account

Failure to verify will result in removal of your profile and reviews.

red_flags.label: Threat to remove profile and reviews

explanation.short: glassdoor-jobs.net is not glassdoor.com — this is a credential phishing page designed to steal your Glassdoor login.
tells: Real Glassdoor communications come from @glassdoor.com — not glassdoor-jobs.net
Glassdoor would display a warning directly in your account dashboard, not suspend you via email alone
The 48-hour urgency and 'verify identity' link pattern is classic phishing
As a job seeker with an active Glassdoor account, this threat is especially designed to motivate action
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to glassdoor.com and log in to check your account status — do not use the email link.
explanation.consequence: The fake login captures your Glassdoor credentials — and if you reuse passwords, many other accounts too.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Account violation notices are always verifiable by logging into the platform directly — never act on email links alone.

message.subject: Confirm your phone number to receive direct deposit
message.body: Hi! To complete your payroll setup, we need to verify your phone number for direct deposit. We'll send a 6-digit code to your phone. Please reply to this email with the code once you receive it so we can finalize your account.

red_flags.label: Asked to email back a code sent to your phone
Payroll setup via email reply, not HR portal

explanation.short: Legitimate payroll departments use secure HR portals — asking you to reply to an email with an SMS code is account takeover.
tells: They triggered a verification code to be sent to your number, and now want you to hand it back via email
The code is not for 'direct deposit setup' — it's the key to whatever account they're targeting
Real payroll onboarding uses platforms like Workday, ADP, or Gusto — not email code exchanges
The .co domain is a budget registration inconsistent with a real corporation's HR system
explanation.safe_move: Do not reply with any code. Contact Vertex Corp directly using a phone number from their official website to verify.
explanation.consequence: The code hands them access to whatever account it was issued for — likely your bank or email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payroll setup never requires you to share a code that was texted to you — that is account takeover, not onboarding.

message.subject: Your Zoomify Pro account for your interview is expiring
message.body:
Your free Zoomify Pro trial for job seekers expires in 24 hours. To continue using Zoomify for your upcoming interviews without interruption, renew at $14.99/month. Act now to avoid reverting to basic — which disables video in interviews.

Renew: zoomify-business.net/renew

red_flags.label: Lookalike of real video platform (not zoomify.com)
Targeted at job seekers — interview disruption fear

explanation.short: zoomify-business.net is a fake — this phishing email exploits job seekers' anxiety about interview tools to steal payment info.
tells: The sender domain is zoomify-business.net, not zoomify.com — always check the actual domain
Threatening to disable video in upcoming interviews targets the specific fear of a job seeker
Renewing through an email link goes to a fake payment page — your card details are captured
Real video conferencing apps manage subscriptions within their own app, not via emailed links
explanation.safe_move: Go to zoomify.com directly to check your subscription status. Do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: Your payment card details are captured by the fake renewal page — no subscription is actually renewed.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Manage all software subscriptions through the official app or website — never through a renewal link in an email.

message.subject: Update on your application to Meridian Analytics
message.body:
Hi Alex,

Thank you for applying to the Data Analyst position at Meridian Analytics. After careful review, we've decided to move forward with other candidates whose experience more closely matches the role.

We'll keep your profile on file and encourage you to apply to future openings.

Best,
TalentHub Recruiting

explanation.short: This is a legitimate rejection email — it comes from talenthub.com, references a specific role, and asks for nothing.
tells: Sent from talenthub.com — the platform's official domain
References a specific company (Meridian Analytics) and role (Data Analyst) consistent with a real application
No links to click, no requests for information, no urgency
Polite, specific, and asks nothing from you — scam emails always want something
explanation.safe_move: No action needed. Keep track of your applications to recognize legitimate follow-ups.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine rejection notice.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate rejection emails come from the platform's domain, reference your specific application, and ask for nothing.

message.subject: Interview Confirmation — Thursday Feb 27 at 2pm EST
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

This is a confirmation for your video interview on Thursday, February 27 at 2:00 PM EST for the Product Manager role at Veritas.

Interview link: cloudmeet.com/xyz-abc-def (CloudMeet)
Duration: 45 minutes
Interviewers: Tara Simmons (HR), Devon Park (Hiring Manager)

Please bring a copy of your resume. Let me know if you need to reschedule.

Tara Simmons | HR Business Partner | Veritas HR

explanation.short: This is a legitimate interview confirmation — it comes from the company's real domain and contains specific, consistent details.
tells: Sent from tsimmons@veritashr.com — a corporate email at the company's domain
Contains specific interviewer names, a real-looking CloudMeet link, and matches a role you presumably applied for
No payment, no credential request, no urgency beyond normal scheduling
Provides a reschedule option — a normal professional courtesy
explanation.safe_move: Add it to your calendar and prepare for the interview. Verify by checking your original application confirmation.
explanation.consequence: No risk — this is a routine interview confirmation.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Interview confirmations from company domains with specific names and a rescheduling option are legitimate — scams don't offer to reschedule.

message.subject: Background check requested by Apex Technologies
message.body:
Apex Technologies has requested a background check as part of your pre-employment process. Please complete the form at sterlingcheck.com/candidate to submit your information securely.

This authorization expires in 7 days. If you have not applied to Apex Technologies, disregard this email.

red_flags.label: Third party asking for personal info

explanation.short: This is a legitimate background check request — Sterling is a real background screening company, and the link goes to sterlingcheck.com.
tells: Sterling Background Services (sterlingcheck.com) is a major background screening company used by many employers
The link goes to sterlingcheck.com — the company's real domain, not a lookalike
Background checks from a third party are standard hiring practice before employment
The email acknowledges you may not have applied ('if you have not applied, disregard') — scams don't do this
explanation.safe_move: If you recently interviewed at Apex Technologies and are expecting this step, proceed at sterlingcheck.com.
explanation.consequence: No risk. If unexpected, verify with your Apex Technologies contact first.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Background checks from known services like Sterling are legitimate — verify the link goes to the screener's official domain.

message.subject: Offer Letter — Product Manager, Veritas
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

Please find attached your offer letter for the Product Manager position at Veritas. Start date: March 10. Salary: $95,000/year plus benefits.

We use Workday for onboarding — you'll receive a separate invite from workday.com within 24 hours to complete your forms.

Please sign via SignVault (a separate email will follow from signvault.com) and return by February 28.

Congratulations!
Derek Walsh | VP of HR | Veritas

explanation.short: This is a legitimate offer letter — from a company domain, referencing established platforms (Workday, SignVault), with specific details matching your interview process.
tells: Sent from dwalsh@veritashr.com — the company's domain, consistent with prior contact
Mentions Workday and SignVault for onboarding — established enterprise platforms with their own domains
Specific salary, start date, and role are consistent with a real offer following an interview
No payment requests, no unusual links — just instructions to use established tools
explanation.safe_move: Sign via the SignVault email that comes from signvault.com. Complete onboarding through the Workday invite from workday.com.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. Verify the SignVault and WorkPortal emails also come from their official domains.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: A real offer letter from a company's domain referencing established HR platforms is legitimate — verify each platform invite separately.

message.body: Hi! A recruiter at Apex Technologies viewed your profile and saved it to their candidate list. Log in at talenthub.com to see your profile activity and message them directly if you're interested.

red_flags.label: Platform DM with link to site

explanation.short: This is a legitimate TalentHub platform notification — it comes from their verified account and directs you to talenthub.com.
tells: Sent from @talenthub_official — a verified platform account, not a personal-style handle
Directs you to talenthub.com — the platform's own domain
No request for personal info, no urgency, no payment
Profile activity notifications are a standard job platform feature
explanation.safe_move: Log in to talenthub.com to view your profile activity and reach out to the recruiter if interested.
explanation.consequence: No risk — routine job platform activity notification.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate job platform notifications come from verified accounts and direct you only to the platform's official domain.

message.subject: Unusual direct deposit detected on your account
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

We noticed a direct deposit of $3,800 from PINNACLE-STAFFING was posted to your account today — which is outside your usual deposit history.

If this was expected (e.g., a new employer or freelance payment), no action is needed.

If this is unexpected, please call the number on the back of your debit card immediately.

BluePeak Security Team

red_flags.label: Unexpected large deposit from unknown source

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank security notice — it comes from bluepeak.com and directs you to your card's back number, not a link.
tells: Sent from security@bluepeak.com — the bank's official domain
Asks you to call the number on your card — not a separate number in the email
Acknowledges a legitimate scenario (new employer) to help you assess before acting
No embedded link to click, no urgency beyond 'if unexpected, call your bank'
explanation.safe_move: If the deposit is from a new employer or side work, no action needed. If unexpected, call BluePeak using the number on your card.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. An unexpected deposit could indicate a check scam setup — if uncertain, call your bank.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank security emails direct you to your card's back number — they do not include emergency call-now links.

message.subject: Password reset requested for your account
message.body:
We received a request to reset the password for your JobBridge account (jordan@email.com). If you requested this, click the link below within 1 hour:

jobbridge.com/reset?token=a9f2c...

If you did not request this, no action is needed — your current password remains unchanged.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate password reset email — it comes from jobbridge.com, only acts if you requested it, and assures you no action is needed if you didn't.
tells: Sent from security@jobbridge.com — the platform's official domain
The reset link goes to jobbridge.com — the platform's own domain with a reset token
Explicitly states 'if you did not request this, no action is needed' — phishing emails never say this
1-hour token expiry is standard security practice for password resets
explanation.safe_move: If you initiated the reset, use the link. If not, ignore it — your password hasn't changed.
explanation.consequence: No risk. If you didn't request a reset, someone may be trying your email on the platform — consider enabling two-factor authentication.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate password reset emails offer a clear 'if you didn't do this, ignore it' option — phishing emails create panic instead.

message.subject: Your resume optimizer subscription renews today — $189/year
message.body: Your CareerBoost Pro annual subscription will renew for $189 today. If you did not authorize this charge, call our cancellation team at 1-866-555-0412 within 6 hours to receive a refund.

red_flags.label: Surprise charge — must call to cancel
Exploits job seeker fear of missing a paid tool

explanation.short: This fake billing notice targets job seekers who may have trialed resume tools — the phone number is the trap.
tells: careerboost-renewals.net is not careerboost.com — real companies bill from their own domain
Calling the 'cancellation team' connects to scammers who ask for remote access to 'process the refund'
Job seekers commonly use resume tools, making this a targeted, plausible-sounding scam
Real subscription renewal emails show the last 4 digits of the card being charged
explanation.safe_move: Do not call. Check your bank statement. If you have a CareerBoost account, manage billing at careerboost.com directly.
explanation.consequence: Calling leads to a refund scam where they ask for remote computer access and steal banking credentials.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never call a number in a billing email about an unexpected charge — verify all billing through the company's official website.

message.subject: Your I-9 Employment Eligibility Verification is pending
message.body: Your employer has initiated an I-9 verification with USCIS. You are required to complete your electronic I-9 form within 3 business days or your employment authorization will be placed on hold. Complete at: uscis-employment-verify.com/i9

explanation.short: USCIS is a U.S. government agency — all official communications come from .gov domains, never .com.
tells: uscis-employment-verify.com is not uscis.gov — government agencies always use .gov
Real I-9 forms are completed in person or through an employer's HR system, not a random website
Threatening to put employment authorization 'on hold' creates panic targeting new job seekers
USCIS does not directly email employees to complete forms — employers manage this process
explanation.safe_move: Contact your employer's HR department directly to verify the I-9 process. Complete the form through your employer, not a third-party link.
explanation.consequence: The fake I-9 form captures your Social Security number, ID documents, and other identity information.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All USCIS and government employment forms use .gov domains — any .com claiming to be a government agency is fake.

message.subject: Your home office equipment has shipped
message.body:
Hi Jordan,

Your order from WorkGear Supply (Order #SW-98812) has shipped and is estimated to arrive Thursday, Feb 27.

Tracking: SW8819922 — Track at swiftship.com/track

If you did not place this order, contact WorkGear Supply at workgear.com/support.

SwiftShip

explanation.short: This is a legitimate shipping notification — from swiftship.com, with an order number, and a redirect to the merchant for disputes.
tells: Sent from tracking@swiftship.com — the carrier's official domain
Contains specific order number and tracking number consistent with a real shipment
Track link goes to swiftship.com — no third-party domain
If the package is for new job equipment you ordered, this is routine
explanation.safe_move: Track the package at swiftship.com. If you don't recognize the order, contact WorkGear Supply at workgear.com.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate shipping emails come from the carrier's official domain and include specific order numbers — no fees or login required.

message.subject: Updated banking details for future payments
message.body:
Dear Valued Client,

Please be advised that SupplyCore has updated its banking details effective immediately. Kindly update your records and ensure all future payments are directed to:

Bank: PinnacleBank
Account: 8847221903
Routing: 021000021

Please confirm receipt of this email.

Kind regards,
Accounts Receivable — SupplyCore Ltd

red_flags.label: Bank account change requested via email only
supplycore-ltd.net vs your vendor's real domain
Asks you to confirm receipt — validates active email

explanation.short: Vendor bank account changes should always be verified by phone call to a known number — never actioned from an email alone.
tells: Business Email Compromise (BEC) works by intercepting or spoofing vendor email to redirect payments
The domain supplycore-ltd.net may differ from your actual vendor's domain by a small change
Legitimate vendors establish banking change procedures in contracts — not a one-off email
Calling your vendor's known number (not any number in this email) is the only safe verification
explanation.safe_move: Do not update banking details. Call SupplyCore directly using the number from your contract or prior invoices to verify.
explanation.consequence: Your next payment goes to the scammer's account. BEC losses average $130,000 per incident for small businesses.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any vendor banking change must be verified by phone to a known contact — email alone is never sufficient authorization.

message.subject: Urgent — wire needed today
message.body: Hi Sarah, I need you to process an urgent wire transfer of $22,500 to a new vendor before close of business today. This is time sensitive for a deal we're closing. I'm in back-to-back meetings — please handle and confirm when done. I'll explain later. Thanks.

red_flags.label: CEO requesting urgent wire via email
No invoice, PO, or vendor verification info
'In meetings' — unavailable to verify by phone

explanation.short: CEO fraud (whaling) follows this exact script: urgent wire, no documentation, unavailable for verification, deadline pressure.
tells: Legitimate wire transfers follow an approval process with invoices, POs, and dual authorization
The .co domain is a common giveaway — scammers register lookalike domains differing by one character
'In meetings' is specifically designed to prevent you from calling to verify
No legitimate CEO authorizes a $22,500 wire with no supporting documentation via a single email
explanation.safe_move: Do not wire anything. Call or text your CEO on their known personal number to verify before any action.
explanation.consequence: Once wired, funds are typically moved internationally within hours and are unrecoverable.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Always verify wire requests by phone to a known number — no matter how senior the requestor appears to be.

message.subject: Invoice #INV-2024-8812 — Due in 3 days
message.body:
Please find attached Invoice #INV-2024-8812 for $1,247.00 for office supplies delivered February 10. Payment is due within 3 days to avoid a 5% late fee. Pay at: officepro-supplies-invoice.com/pay

Thank you for your business.

red_flags.label: Invoice with no reference to a purchase order
Direct payment link in invoice email

explanation.short: Fake invoice attacks work by sending plausible-looking invoices hoping someone in accounting pays without cross-checking a PO.
tells: The domain officepro-supplies-invoice.com is a fake — your vendor's real billing domain would differ
Match every invoice to a purchase order — no PO reference is a major red flag in AP processes
Legitimate invoices from established vendors don't include direct payment links with 3-day deadlines
Sending to the accounting department hoping someone pays without verification is the core of this attack
explanation.safe_move: Match the invoice to a purchase order before paying. Call OfficePro directly using your existing vendor contact to verify.
explanation.consequence: Payment goes to the scammer. Without PO matching, businesses commonly pay fraudulent invoices.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Every invoice should match a purchase order before payment — no PO match, no payment.

message.subject: Mandatory Safety Compliance Filing — $1,200 Penalty Pending
message.body: Your business has been flagged for a missing annual OSHA safety compliance filing. A $1,200 civil penalty is pending. To avoid this penalty and a possible audit, complete your compliance submission and pay the filing fee of $299 at osha-regulatory-notice.com/filing within 10 days.

red_flags.label: Pay a fee to avoid a penalty — classic scam
No specific filing or business identifier cited

explanation.short: OSHA is a federal agency — all communications come from osha.gov, and federal penalties are never resolved by paying a fee to a .com website.
tells: All OSHA communications come from osha.gov — any .com claiming to be OSHA is fake
Real OSHA violations include your business name, EIN, specific violations cited, and a formal citation
Paying a 'filing fee' to avoid a penalty is a classic scam structure — no agency works this way
The vague filing claim is designed to be plausible for any business without being verifiable
explanation.safe_move: Ignore. If concerned about OSHA compliance, contact OSHA directly at osha.gov or 1-800-321-OSHA.
explanation.consequence: You pay $299, receive no protection from any penalty (which didn't exist), and your payment details are captured.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Federal agency enforcement always comes from .gov sources — never pay a government penalty through a .com website link.

message.subject: Request to update my direct deposit
message.body: Hi, I recently opened a new bank account and need to update my direct deposit information before the next payroll run. New account: BluePeak Bank, Account 9912004471, Routing 021000021. Please update before Friday. Thank you!

red_flags.label: Direct deposit change requested via email
No request through official HR system

explanation.short: Payroll diversion fraud works by impersonating an employee requesting a bank change just before payday.
tells: Real payroll updates are processed through HR systems (ADP, Gusto, etc.) — not email requests
The .co domain likely spoofs the real company domain by one character
Timing just before payroll is deliberate — it creates urgency and ensures a large payment goes to the scammer
Verify any direct deposit change by calling the employee at their known phone number
explanation.safe_move: Call the employee at their known number to verify before changing payroll information.
explanation.consequence: An entire paycheck is deposited to the scammer's account on the next payroll run.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All payroll changes must be initiated through your HR portal and verified verbally — email alone is never sufficient.

message.subject: Action Required: Verify your BizListings to prevent suspension
message.body:
Your BizListings profile for Acme Plumbing is at risk of suspension. Unverified listings are removed after 30 days. Re-verify your listing now to keep your reviews and ranking active.

Verify at: bizlistings-verify.com/listing?id=29847

red_flags.label: Threat to remove reviews and ranking
Uses your business name to appear legitimate
Verify link doesn't go to bizlistings.com

explanation.short: BizListings notices come from bizlistings.com — this domain is a fake designed to steal your BizListings account credentials.
tells: bizlistings-verify.com is not bizlistings.com — real BizListings communications always come from bizlistings.com or bizlistings.com/business
Using your actual business name makes the email appear targeted and legitimate — scammers harvest these from BizDirectory
The 'verify listing' link leads to a fake BizListings login page to capture your credentials
Manage your BizListings directly at bizlistings.com/business
explanation.safe_move: Go to bizlistings.com/business directly and check your profile status. Do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: The fake login captures your BizListings account credentials — giving access to your Gmail, Drive, and business systems.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: All BizListings services use bizlistings.com domains — any business verification email from another domain is a phishing attempt.

message.subject: Your BooksOnline account has been locked — action required
message.body:
We've detected unusual activity on your BooksOnline accounting account. To protect your financial data, we've temporarily locked access. Unlock your account at: booksonline-help.net/unlock

Failure to verify within 24 hours will result in account termination and possible data loss.

explanation.short: Accounting software phishing is high-value — your bookkeeping credentials give access to your entire financial history and bank connections.
tells: booksonline-help.net is not booksonline.com — real accounting software notifies through the app itself
Threatening data loss (your entire accounting history) is specifically calibrated to provoke immediate action
Real lockout notifications include the last login IP and guide you to the official site — not an email link
Accounting credentials are the highest-value target for small business fraud
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to booksonline.com and log in to check account status. Do not use the email link.
explanation.consequence: Your bookkeeping credentials give access to bank feeds, vendor payment details, payroll data, and years of financial records.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Accounting software issues are always resolved through the official app or website — never through an email unlock link.

message.subject: Delinquent Sales Tax Notice — Immediate Payment Required
message.body: Our records indicate your business owes $3,419.00 in unpaid sales tax for Q3. Failure to remit within 5 days will result in account levy, license suspension, and referral to our collections agency. Pay online: state-tax-compliance-dept.com/pay

red_flags.label: Not your state's actual tax authority domain
Threat of levy and license suspension

explanation.short: State tax authorities use .gov domains — a .com claiming to be your state's Department of Revenue is always fake.
tells: Every state's Department of Revenue uses a .gov domain (e.g., revenue.state.tx.us, tax.ny.gov)
Real tax delinquency notices arrive by certified mail with specific filing periods and penalty breakdowns
Paying through a .com link sends money to scammers, not the actual state government
Threats of levy and license suspension are designed to panic business owners into immediate payment
explanation.safe_move: Verify by visiting your state's official tax authority website directly or calling their published number.
explanation.consequence: Payment goes to scammers. Your real tax account remains unaffected (and may still have a balance).
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: State and federal tax notices arrive by certified mail — never pay a tax bill through a link in an email.

message.subject: Your NovaSoft 365 license is expiring — remote session required
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 for Business license expires in 48 hours due to a billing issue. To prevent service interruption, our technician needs to connect remotely to renew your license. Schedule your 15-minute session: novasoft-365-support.net/schedule

Note: This affects all users in your organization.

red_flags.label: Remote access required to renew license

explanation.short: No software vendor needs remote access to your computer to renew a license — this is a remote access scam targeting business admins.
tells: Software license renewals are handled through vendor portals, not remote support sessions
novasoft-365-support.net is not novasoft.com — one of the most common IT impersonation patterns
Saying 'all users affected' multiplies urgency — a common business-targeted pressure tactic
Remote access to an admin workstation gives attackers access to your entire network
explanation.safe_move: Manage your NovaSoft 365 subscription at novasoft.com/admin. Do not grant remote access from an unsolicited email.
explanation.consequence: Remote access to an admin account enables credential theft, ransomware installation, and network-wide compromise.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Software license renewals never require remote access — manage all renewals through the vendor's official admin portal.

message.subject: Notice of Contract Dispute — Action Required
message.body:
Our client has engaged us regarding an outstanding contract dispute with your business. Please review the attached notice of claim (DOC file) and respond within 10 days.

Failure to respond may result in legal action. Enable all document features to view the full claim.

Peterson & Associates LLP

red_flags.label: Law firm name unverifiable from .co domain
DOC attachment from unknown law firm

explanation.short: 'Enable all document features' in a Word file is malware delivery — real law firms send PDFs, not macro-enabled documents.
tells: Legitimate legal notices are sent as PDFs, not macro-enabled Word documents
'Enable all document features' is the standard malware trigger — it runs embedded VBA code
Threatening legal action creates the panic that makes people click without thinking
Real law firms would serve formal notice through registered mail, not .co email attachments
explanation.safe_move: Do not open the attachment. If you believe there may be a real dispute, search for Peterson & Associates independently and contact them through verified means.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros installs ransomware or a remote access tool — potentially encrypting your entire business network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never enable macros or document features in a file received by email — this is malware delivery, 100% of the time.

message.subject: FINAL NOTICE: yourbusiness.com domain expires in 5 days
message.body:
This is a final notice that yourbusiness.com expires in 5 days. Failure to renew will result in domain loss and potential acquisition by competitors. Renew now for $79/year: domainguard-register.com/renew

Act now to protect your online presence.

red_flags.label: Threat of domain loss and competitor acquisition

explanation.short: Domain renewal notices must come from your actual registrar — third-party domain 'expiry' notices are scams designed to transfer your domain and overcharge.
tells: Your domain registrar is already known to you (GoDaddy, Namecheap, etc.) — not DomainGuard
Paying this 'renewal' may transfer your domain to a different registrar at an inflated price
Domains typically cost $10–$15/year — $79 is a massive markup
Scammers harvest domain expiry data and mass-send these notices hoping business owners panic
explanation.safe_move: Log in to your actual domain registrar's website to check your domain's real expiry date. Renew only there.
explanation.consequence: You pay $79 and may inadvertently transfer your domain to a predatory registrar, losing control of your web presence.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Domain renewals come only from your registrar — ignore all domain notices from companies you don't recognize.

message.subject: Authorize new compliance app for your organization
message.body:
Your NovaSoft 365 admin account requires authorization of our new Compliance Monitoring app to meet updated security standards. This app requires access to: all user emails, file storage, calendar events, and admin settings.

Authorize for your organization: novasoft365-admin.net/authorize

red_flags.label: Requests all email, files, calendar, and admin access
Org-wide OAuth consent via email link

explanation.short: An OAuth grant with access to all email, files, and admin settings from a lookalike domain is a complete organizational account takeover.
tells: novasoft365-admin.net is not novasoft.com — NovaSoft would use their own domain for app authorizations
Legitimate compliance apps are deployed through the admin portal (admin.novasoft.com), not email links
The permission scope requested (all email + files + admin) is essentially full organizational access
'Required for compliance' is an invented pretext — real compliance changes are announced with documentation
explanation.safe_move: Do not authorize. Log in to your admin portal at novasoft.com to manage app permissions.
explanation.consequence: Authorizing gives attackers persistent access to your entire organization's email, files, and admin controls — even after password changes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Never authorize third-party app access through an email link — all app permissions are managed in the admin portal.

message.subject: Consumer complaint filed against your business — response required
message.body:
A consumer complaint (Case #BBB-2024-04419) has been filed against your business. You have 7 days to respond or your BBB accreditation will be suspended. View and respond to the complaint: bbb-complaint-portal.org/case/04419

Immediate response is required to maintain your rating.

explanation.short: The BBB sends all communications from bbb.org — any BBB complaint notice from another domain is a fake.
tells: The BBB's official domain is bbb.org — all legitimate complaint management happens at bbb.org
Clicking the link on bbb-complaint-portal.org leads to a phishing page or malware download
Real BBB complaints include your business's specific accreditation details — this is generic
Threatening accreditation suspension is specifically designed to pressure businesses into clicking
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to bbb.org and search for any complaints against your business using your business name.
explanation.consequence: The link may harvest your credentials or install malware — and your BBB reputation may be researched to make follow-up scams more convincing.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The BBB only communicates through bbb.org — any other domain claiming to be the BBB is fake.

message.subject: Purchase inquiry — I'll pay via certified check
message.body: Hi, I'm interested in the commercial equipment you listed for $3,200. I'll send a certified check for $5,500 — please keep your fee and use the remaining $2,300 to pay my shipping coordinator who will pick up the item. Let me know if this works!

red_flags.label: Certified checks can be faked and bounce
Buyer using personal Gmail for business purchase

explanation.short: This is a check overpayment scam — the certified check will bounce after you've already wired $2,300 to the 'shipping coordinator.'
tells: Certified and cashier's checks can be professionally counterfeited — they appear to clear for 1–2 weeks before bouncing
The shipping coordinator is the scammer — your $2,300 goes directly to them
No legitimate buyer needs to overpay and have you wire back the difference
Business equipment buyers typically pay via ACH, wire, or escrow — not certified check to a stranger
explanation.safe_move: Decline. For business equipment sales, use a secure payment method like wire transfer where funds are verified first.
explanation.consequence: The check bounces after you've sent $2,300. Your bank holds you liable for the full check amount.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Any buyer who overpays and asks you to forward the difference to a third party is running a check scam.

message.subject: RE: New vendor payment
message.body: Hi — I need you to process a payment to a new vendor for a project I'm handling. $14,800 wire to NexaTrade LLC, account 7712004591, routing 021000089. This needs to go out today and I need confirmation ASAP. Don't discuss with anyone — this is confidential until the deal closes.

red_flags.label: New vendor with no invoice or PO

explanation.short: The secrecy instruction ('don't discuss with anyone') is the clearest signal — it's designed to prevent verification of what is a CEO fraud attack.
tells: Legitimate wire transfers have invoices, purchase orders, and can withstand internal discussion
Confidentiality demands are specifically used to stop you from calling your CEO to verify
The .co domain is a one-character difference from the real company domain — a classic BEC technique
Any wire over a few thousand dollars should require dual authorization and documented business justification
explanation.safe_move: Do not wire anything. Call your CEO directly on their known phone number to verify — regardless of the confidentiality request.
explanation.consequence: Funds are wired internationally within hours. Recovery rate for BEC wire fraud is under 15% even with rapid FBI reporting.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Confidentiality requests in payment emails are red flags, not permissions — always verify large wire requests by phone.

message.subject: Unusual login attempt on your PayStream account
message.body:
We detected a sign-in attempt to your PayStream merchant account from an unrecognized device. To secure your account and prevent unauthorized transactions, please verify your identity within 4 hours.

Verify: paystream-security.net/merchant-verify

Ignoring this notice may result in account suspension.

explanation.short: Payment processor credentials are high-value targets — paystream-security.net is a fake designed to steal your merchant account login.
tells: Real PayStream security notices come from paystream.com — not paystream-security.net
Merchant account phishing is highly targeted at small businesses — it gives access to payment processing and business banking
Real payment processors alert via in-app notifications and direct you to the official site
4-hour windows are designed to prevent careful evaluation
explanation.safe_move: Go directly to paystream.com and log in to check account activity. Do not use the link in the email.
explanation.consequence: Your merchant account credentials allow the attacker to redirect payouts, process refunds to their cards, or access linked business banking.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Payment processor security alerts are always verified through the official site — never through an email link.

message.subject: Invoice #SC-2024-1109 — $892.40 due March 15
message.body:
Dear Acme Plumbing,

Please find attached Invoice #SC-2024-1109 for $892.40 for pipe fittings delivered February 8 (PO #ACM-2024-0091).

Payment is due March 15 to our account on file. No changes to our banking information.

Questions? Contact us at accounts@supplycore.com or 1-800-555-0210.

SupplyCore Accounts

explanation.short: This is a legitimate invoice — from the vendor's real domain, referencing a specific PO, and explicitly noting no banking changes.

References a specific PO number (ACM-2024-0091) that should match your purchase records
Explicitly states 'no changes to our banking information' — a signal a real vendor might include after BEC awareness
Payment terms are normal (net 30-ish), with a direct vendor contact for questions
explanation.safe_move: Match the invoice to PO #ACM-2024-0091 in your system and pay to the account already on file for SupplyCore.
explanation.consequence: No risk. Verify the PO number matches before approving payment.

message.body: PinnacleBank Business: Outgoing wire of $14,500 to NexaTrade LLC was submitted. If not authorized by you, call 1-800-555-0100 immediately — this is the number on the back of your business debit card.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate bank wire notification — short code sender, real bank number, and no links or login requests.
tells: Comes from a short code (72791) registered to PinnacleBank's business banking alerts
Provides the number from the back of your debit card as the verification path — not a separate unknown number
No link, no request to reply with anything, no ask for credentials
Wire confirmation texts are a genuine fraud prevention service offered by most business banks
explanation.safe_move: If you authorized the wire, no action needed. If not, call 1-800-555-0100 immediately — time matters for wire fraud.
explanation.consequence: If unauthorized, calling your bank quickly can sometimes halt the wire. Act within minutes.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Legitimate bank wire alerts come from short codes and reference the number on your card — they never include links or ask you to log in.

message.subject: CP2000 Notice — Discrepancy in reported income
message.body:
This is a notification that the NRS has mailed a CP2000 notice to your registered business address regarding a discrepancy in your 2022 business tax return. The notice was mailed February 12.

You do not need to take action in response to this email. Your mailed notice contains all details and response instructions.

NRS.gov — Never share personal tax info by email.

explanation.short: This is a legitimate NRS notification email — it comes from nrs.gov, asks for nothing, and says to wait for the mailed notice.
tells: Sent from do-not-reply@nrs.gov — the NRS's official domain
Explicitly says 'you do not need to take action in response to this email' — no link, no payment, no call
Notifies you a physical letter was mailed — consistent with how the NRS actually operates
Includes a reminder not to share personal tax info by email — a legitimate NRS safety message
explanation.safe_move: Watch for the mailed CP2000 notice and consult a tax professional about the discrepancy.
explanation.consequence: No risk from this email. The CP2000 is a common correspondence notice, not an audit.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: The NRS emails you only to notify — never to collect. All action items from the NRS come by mail, not by email.

message.subject: Request for Quotation — see attached PO
message.body:
Dear Supplier,

We are interested in placing a large order with your company. Please review the attached purchase order and provide pricing. The document requires macros to be enabled to display our full specifications.

We look forward to your response.

GlobalVendors Procurement

explanation.short: Fake RFQ (request for quotation) emails are a common malware delivery vector targeting small businesses — the attachment is the payload.
tells: No legitimate purchase order requires macros to be enabled — real POs are PDFs or plain spreadsheets
An RFQ with no product specifics is a mass-sent phishing attempt — real buyers specify what they need
The .net domain and generic company name are inconsistent with a real procurement department
Enabling macros installs malware immediately on your business network
explanation.safe_move: Delete the email. Do not open the attachment. If a legitimate buyer wants to work with you, they can submit inquiries through your website.
explanation.consequence: Enabling macros delivers ransomware or a remote access tool to your business network.
explanation.behavioral_reinforcement: Unsolicited RFQs requiring macro-enabled documents are malware — real procurement teams use PDFs and don't require macros.
